title: Stepped Hitlists
text: Stepped Hitlists Uniface uses the Select request to create a partially built hitlist called a stepped hitlist. This mechanism ensures that a limited number of occurrences are fetched from the database, even though a large number of occurrences match the retrieve profile entered by the user. The Select request can be called multiple times to pass the matching occurrences in a number of steps. Either the DBMS or the connector maintains a state to determine which records have to still be added to the hitlist. This mechanism increases performance. A partially built hitlist is called a stepped hitlist, because it is built in steps. Not all DBMSs support stepped hitlists, and those that do may not use the default value of ten occurrences in a step. The function $curhits returns the number of occurrences in the hitlist, but not the total number of occurrences matching the retrieve profile. $hits returns the total number of occurrences matching the retrieve profile. The performance benefits of using a stepped hitlist are lost if you use a function like $hits , which results in the complete hitlist being built. If the entity name is given as a parameter with $curhits , the number of occurrences of that entity in the hitlist is returned. This value is negative if the hitlist has only been partially built.This happens when there are more occurrences which match the retrieve profile that are not yet in the hitlist. Note: To avoid confusion when using scroll bars, you are advised to limit the total number of multiple occurrences painted on your forms to one less than the stepped hitlist rate. In other words, if using the default rate of 10, you should paint nine or fewer occurrences on your forms. Dynamic Step Size For some DBMSs, when not retrieving on primary key values, a stepped hitlist uses a dynamic size for each step. This is because the DBMS can only select all records with the same index value (C-ISAM is an example of this). Therefore, if a retrieve profile specifies all records with a foreign key greater than 20, for example, and the first 15 occurrences all have a foreign key of 21, the step size for the stepped hitlist is 15. This means that $curhits returns -15 (if the hitlist is only partially built). Performance $hits The hitlist mechanism is completely under the control of Uniface, with the exception of a few DBMSs for which you can define a preferred step size. You do not need to be concerned about it, except when trying to improve response time. If you refer to $hits in a ProcScript instruction or debug mode, the response time improvements offered by the stepped hitlist are lost. read order by If an order by qualifier is included with the read statement and the DBMS does not support this, Uniface builds the entire hitlist immediately. In general, SQL-based DBMSs support order by , and record-level databases do not. Related Links Stepped Hitlists for Performance Uniface uses a stepped hitlist to improve performance. This mechanism ensures that a limited number of occurrences are fetched from the database, even though a large number of occurrences match the retrieve profile entered by the user. ProcScript instructions and hitlist functionality can affect performance. $curhits Return the number of occurrences in the hitlist. $hits Return the number of occurrences in the hitlist. read Set the access mode.
